summary.intent: Fix GitHub issue #736 where the Nostr channel panics when sync ChannelPlugin trait methods call blocking_read()/blocking_write() on tokio::sync::RwLock from within a tokio runtime. User wanted a failing test added first, then the fix.
summary.outcome: Root cause identified and fixed across 3 files by replacing tokio::sync::RwLock with std::sync::RwLock for AccountState's config and cached_allowlist fields. Three regression tests added; all 25 tests pass and full workspace compiles cleanly. PR created.

repo: Every other channel crate (Telegram, Discord, Slack, WhatsApp) correctly uses std::sync::RwLock for state shared between sync ChannelPlugin trait methods — Nostr was the outlier using tokio::sync::RwLock.
The ChannelPlugin trait has sync methods (account_config, account_config_json, update_account_config) that must not use tokio async locks because they are called from within a tokio runtime context.
The moltis project uses a worktree-based workflow under /Users/penso/.superset/worktrees/moltis/ with beads for issue tracking.

code.finding: SharedConfig and cached_allowlist were typed as tokio::sync::RwLock — changed to std::sync::RwLock to match other channel crates.

Five blocking_read()/blocking_write() calls replaced with sync .read()/.write().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()); type inference required intermediate bindings rather than inline unwrap_or_else closures.

Two .read().await calls replaced with sync reads; std::sync::RwLockReadGuard cannot be held across .await points, so block scoping was required to drop the guard before the await — compiler cannot track drop() calls across match arms with awaits.

workflow: Write the failing test first and confirm the exact panic before applying the fix — this validates the reproduction and pins the regression test to the real failure mode.
std::sync::RwLock unwrap_or_else closures on write guards require explicit intermediate bindings for type inference to work in Rust.
std::sync::RwLockReadGuard held across .await points must use block scoping, not explicit drop() calls, because the compiler cannot track drop() across match arms containing awaits.

friction: Type inference failures with unwrap_or_else closures on std::sync::RwLock guards required switching to intermediate bindings — multiple edit/compile cycles needed.
Forgot to add RwLock to the std::sync import in state.rs after removing the tokio::sync import — caused a compile error requiring another fix pass.
Compiler error for RwLockReadGuard held across .await was not immediately obvious; first attempt used drop() which the compiler could not track across match arms.
open_items: The existing integration tests in crates/nostr/tests/nostr_integration.rs are all #[ignore]d (relay connectivity tests) — these remain untested in CI.
No audit was done of other channel crates to confirm none have the same tokio::sync::RwLock misuse pattern in sync trait methods.
